FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'Through the longitudinal, U.S.-based Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, youth (mage=14; n=1415) self-reported their typical smartphone use and passively recorded three weeks of smartphone use via the ABCD-specific Effortless Assessment Research System (EARS) application.'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone use variables via EARS: 'we describe and validate passively-sensed smartphone keyboard and app use measures' and 'Keyboard and app-use measures were reliable and positively correlated' (r=0.33). Also self-reported smartphone use measured.

FT_IC3.evidence: No reported association between smartphone/SM variable and non-SM outcomes. Paper provides descriptive statistics and validates EARS measures but does not report inferential tests linking smartphone use to any outcome variable. It is a measurement/validation paper only.

FT_IC4.evidence: While numeric values are reported (correlations r=0.33, r=0.21-0.35), these are only for validation of EARS vs self-report concordance, not for smartphone/SM → outcome associations.

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a measurement/validation paper without empirical analysis of external outcomes. Does not report phone/SM ↔ non-SM associations. Fails FT-IC3 criterion. Per E5, measurement papers must report external outcome to count.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published empirical research article in Scientific Reports (2024), not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable as paper does not meet FT-IC3 and FT-IC5. Paper is measurement/validation-only without any analyses using phone/SM as dependent or independent variable in relation to non-SM outcomes.
notes: Paper fails full-text screening at FT-IC3/FT-IC5: measurement/validation paper with no external outcome association reported. Only reports EARS-self-report concordance (FT-IC5 NOT_MET per E5).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'The Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, involving over 11,000 youth and their families, is a groundbreaking project examining various factors impacting brain and cognitive development.'

FT_IC2.evidence: No measurement of specific smartphone or social media variables. This is a resource/documentation paper describing the NowIKnowMyABCD help platform and discussion board for ABCD researchers. No phone/SM exposure measurement in this paper.

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential statistical tests linking any variable to outcomes. This is a resource description paper with no empirical analyses.

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric statistics (β, OR, p-values) reported for any associations.

FT_IC5.evidence: Not original empirical analysis. This is a resource/tool description paper documenting NowIKnowMyABCD platform. Per E10, describes a resource with no original analysis.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience (2024), not a preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable; paper fails FT-IC2, FT-IC3, FT-IC5 criteria. No phone/SM measurement and no empirical analysis present.
notes: Excluded: resource/documentation paper describing ABCD help platform. Fails FT-IC2 (no phone/SM measurement), FT-IC3 (no statistical tests), FT-IC5 (no original empirical analysis).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'N = 11,868 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study followed from ages ~9/10 to ~13/14.' Methods state 'We used data from the ABCD longitudinal cohort study. ABCD study recruited youth and parents at 21 United States sites.'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures multiple specific smartphone and social media variables: 'We examined youths' self-reported hours per day (hr/day) of technology subtypes: TV/Movies, video games, YouTube, social media, video chat, and texting.' Social media specifically measured as 'social media' outcome; texting on cell phone; video chat; YouTube (all phone-related).

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports multilevel logistic regression and correlation tests linking technology subtypes to sociodemographic outcomes: 'Using child-reported hours of technology use, increases in the subject-specific odds of using a technology >2 hr/day ranged from 25% (YouTube; 95% CI: 1.16–1.35) to 234% (social media; 95% CI: 3.14–3.55).' Also reports associations with biological sex, parental education, income, marital status via multilevel logistic regression.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric statistics reported: ORs with 95% CIs (e.g., 'social media; 95% CI: 3.14–3.55'), correlation coefficients (rs range: 0.13–0.29), test statistics throughout Table 2.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis: 'We used multilevel logistic regression to examine participant-specific changes and between-subject heterogeneity in the probability of engaging with each technology for more than two hours per day.'

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024), not a preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 APPLIES. Phone/SM (social media, texting, video chat, YouTube, etc.) are treated as DEPENDENT variables in analyses. Predictors are ONLY sociodemographic (biological sex, parent education, income, marital status). Analyses are LONGITUDINAL/cross-sectional (predicting technology use by baseline demographics over time from ages 9/10 to 13/14). No individual psychological/clinical predictors (no ADHD, depression, anxiety, etc.) reported. Meets all three FT-EC7 criteria (a, b, c).
notes: Passes FT-IC1 through FT-IC6. FT-EC7 applies: longitudinal sociodemographic predictors of phone/SM use as DV with no individual-level psychological or clinical predictors. Should be excluded per FT-EC7 criterion.
